text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY CLINIC
FOLLOW-UP NOTE — POST-CHEMORADIOTHERAPY (~6 WEEKS)

ATTENDING: Head and Neck Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Scheduled post-treatment follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Margaret Chen is a 52-year-old Taiwanese-American never-smoking female who presents for her approximately 6-week post-treatment follow-up after completing definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT for HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, stage III (T2N1M0). She was previously admitted approximately 2 weeks post-treatment for dehydration and pain management and was discharged with PEG tube feeds at goal and a plan for close follow-up. She returns today for clinical reassessment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen completed her 7-week course of intensity-modulated radiation therapy (70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-risk volume, SIB technique) with concurrent weekly cisplatin (40 mg/m2 x 6 of 7 planned cycles; cumulative dose approximately 390 mg/m2; one cycle omitted in week 6 due to transient creatinine elevation, then administered in week 7). Treatment was completed without hospitalization during the active treatment period, though she was admitted for 3 days approximately 2 weeks post-completion for worsening mucositis pain, inability to manage secretions, and dehydration. She was discharged on PEG tube feeds at goal rate, an oxycodone taper, and was referred to outpatient speech and swallowing therapy.

Since that hospitalization, she reports gradual but steady improvement. Specifically:

- PAIN/MUCOSITIS: She has weaned off the long-acting oxycodone entirely and is now managing with acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hours as needed and occasional ibuprofen. She rates her throat pain as a 2-3/10 at baseline, up to 4/10 with swallowing attempts. This is a dramatic improvement from her pain scores of 7-8/10 during the hospitalization.

- ORAL INTAKE: She has made meaningful progress. She is taking soft foods — scrambled eggs, yogurt, mashed potatoes, soft fish — and thick liquids. She estimates she is meeting approximately 40-50% of her caloric needs orally. The PEG tube continues to supplement the remainder. She is tolerating tube feeds at goal rate overnight without nausea or discomfort.

- DRY MOUTH: Ongoing and bothersome. She is using artificial saliva spray (Biotene) and sipping water constantly throughout the day. She notes it is better than at the end of treatment but still significantly impairs her enjoyment of food and eating.

- SWALLOWING THERAPY: She has attended 3 sessions of speech and swallowing therapy since discharge and finds the exercises helpful. A modified barium swallow (MBS) has been ordered and is scheduled for the coming week.

- SKIN: The bilateral neck radiation dermatitis has largely resolved. She notes mild skin darkening and dryness in the treated areas.

- WEIGHT/NUTRITION: She has noted a slight uptick in weight compared to discharge (per her home scale). She denies nausea or vomiting. No diarrhea.

- HEARING: She endorses mild high-frequency difficulty that she attributes to the cisplatin, consistent with the grade 1 ototoxicity identified on audiogram during treatment. No tinnitus. She has not noticed progression since completing treatment.

- FATIGUE: Moderate. She has been up and around the house and has taken a few short walks, but is not yet back to her full baseline activity level.

- NECK: She has noticed that the enlarged lymph node she originally felt in her left neck is no longer palpable. She finds this reassuring.

She denies new neck masses, new throat pain, hemoptysis, dyspnea, or other symptoms to suggest recurrence or new complications. She is living at home, supported by her husband, and she appreciates the improvement in her overall condition week by week.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: Xerostomia (persistent grade 2), odynophagia with swallowing (improving, grade 1-2), dysgeusia (grade 2, significant taste alteration), moderate fatigue (grade 1), mild neck skin hyperpigmentation.
Negative: No fever, no chills, no hemoptysis, no shortness of breath, no chest pain, no new neck swelling, no nasal bleeding, no ear pain, no trismus symptoms, no numbness or tingling of the jaw/face, no tinnitus, no aspiration events, no nausea, no vomiting.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, RR 14, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air.
Weight: 57.8 kg (up from 57.0 kg at end of treatment; baseline 62 kg; total treatment-related weight loss ~4.2 kg, approximately 6.8% from baseline).

General: Alert, pleasant, well-appearing female in no acute distress. Slightly fatigued-appearing.
HEENT:
  - Oral cavity: Oropharyngeal mucosa mildly erythematous without frank pseudomembranes; the confluent mucositis from peak treatment has largely resolved; scattered areas of mild erythema at the base of tongue and soft palate, consistent with healing. Saliva appears scant; mucosa somewhat dry. No obvious mucosal irregularity or mass lesion visible on limited oral exam.
  - Tongue: Mildly coated; moves symmetrically; no apparent mass.
  - Dentition: Intact, fluoride trays in use per dental recommendation.
  - Neck: Left and right neck with mild cutaneous hyperpigmentation and subtle skin thickening in the irradiated fields, consistent with late-phase radiation dermatitis grade 1. No moist desquamation. Previously palpable left level IIA lymph node is NO LONGER PALPABLE on careful bimanual examination. No other cervical lymphadenopathy appreciated. No tenderness to palpation over the submandibular or parotid regions.
  - Jaw: Opening estimated at approximately 36-37 mm; no significant trismus at this time.
PEG site: Clean, no erythema, no granulation tissue, no signs of infection. Site well-healed.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Neurological: Cranial nerves grossly intact; shoulder shrug symmetric; tongue protrusion midline.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
Most recent labs (obtained at today's visit):
- CBC: WBC 5.8 x10^9/L (ANC 3.4 x10^9/L, normal), Hgb 11.9 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, improving from nadir during treatment), Plt 238 x10^9/L
- CMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 103, CO2 25, BUN 12, Cr 0.74 mg/dL (normalized; back to baseline), Glucose 94
- Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (low-normal; continuing oral magnesium supplementation)
- TSH: 3.2 mIU/L (normal; patient on pre-existing levothyroxine 50 mcg/day)
- Total protein/albumin: Albumin 3.5 g/dL (borderline-low, consistent with recent nutritional stress; improving)

---

IMAGING:
No new imaging performed at this visit. Re-staging PET-CT and MRI neck are planned for approximately 10-12 weeks post-completion of chemoradiotherapy per head and neck oncology surveillance protocol. The clinical finding of a non-palpable left neck node is an encouraging early sign, though definitive response assessment will await post-treatment imaging.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL SCC (LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, T2N1M0) — STATUS POST DEFINITIVE CONCURRENT CISPLATIN-IMRT:
   Status: Approximately 6 weeks post-treatment completion. Cancer status indeterminate pending formal restaging imaging; clinical picture reassuring (previously palpable left neck node no longer palpable, no new concerning symptoms). Definitive response assessment will be made at 10-12 week post-treatment imaging (PET-CT + MRI neck). Given her favorable biology (HPV+, p16+ strongly diffuse, never-smoker, T2N1M0, ECOG 0 at presentation, no extranodal extension), I have high clinical optimism for a complete response. She is counseled to call immediately if new neck swelling, increased throat pain, bleeding, or other alarming symptoms arise.
   Plan: Schedule post-treatment PET-CT and MRI neck for approximately week 10-12 post-treatment. Obtain imaging results at next clinic visit.

2. MUCOSITIS — RESOLVING (NOW GRADE 1):
   Significant improvement from peak grade 3. Mucosa showing expected healing trajectory. The acute pseudomembranous mucositis has resolved; residual erythema and sensitivity are expected to continue improving over the next 4-6 weeks.
   Plan: Continue salt-and-baking-soda oral rinses twice daily; continue acetaminophen as needed; avoid NSAIDs if adequate renal recovery confirmed. Can discontinue magic mouthwash as pain is now manageable with acetaminophen.

3. DYSPHAGIA — GRADE 2-3, IMPROVING, PEG TUBE DEPENDENT:
   Oral intake improving but not yet sufficient to discontinue tube feeding. Approx 40-50% caloric intake orally; PEG supplementing the rest. Modified barium swallow scheduled. Speech therapy ongoing and progressing.
   Plan: Continue PEG tube feeds at current rate; advance oral diet as tolerated under SLP guidance; MBS to assess swallowing safety and guide diet texture advancement; goal to wean PEG dependence over coming weeks as oral intake improves. PEG removal to be considered when patient sustains ≥80-85% of caloric needs orally with stable weight.

4. XEROSTOMIA — GRADE 2 (PERSISTENT):
   Expected late effect of bilateral neck irradiation; parotid gland sparing was performed per planning constraints but some parotid dose was unavoidable given nodal volumes. Pilocarpine 5 mg TID was not yet started — will initiate at this visit.
   Plan: Prescribe pilocarpine (Salagen) 5 mg TID with meals; counsel regarding cholinergic side effects (sweating, flushing); continue Biotene spray and frequent water sips; avoid mouth-drying medications (antihistamines, anticholinergics).

5. DYSGEUSIA — GRADE 2:
   Significant taste alteration persisting. Expected post-radiation and post-cisplatin effect. Reassure patient that taste recovery occurs gradually over months; majority of patients see substantial improvement by 12 months.
   Plan: Expectant management; zinc supplementation discussed (limited evidence but low risk — will defer to patient preference); reinforce adequate nutritional intake despite taste alteration.

6. RADIATION DERMATITIS — GRADE 1 (NEARLY RESOLVED):
   Mild hyperpigmentation and skin texture changes remain; no moist desquamation.
   Plan: Gentle skin care; mild moisturizer (CeraVe or similar); sunscreen protection to treated skin areas; no specific intervention required.

7. HYPOMAGNESEMIA — GRADE 1 (PERSISTENT, IMPROVING):
   Mg 1.8 mEq/L, continuing to trend toward normal. Likely reflects ongoing cisplatin-related tubular effects.
   Plan: Continue oral magnesium oxide 400 mg twice daily; recheck Mg at next visit in 3-4 weeks.

8. ANEMIA (MILD) — LIKELY TREATMENT-RELATED:
   Hgb 11.9 g/dL, normocytic; likely related to cisplatin and nutritional status during treatment. No transfusion required.
   Plan: Expectant management; dietary iron and protein optimization; recheck CBC at next visit.

9. HYPOTHYROIDISM (PRE-EXISTING, ON LEVOTHYROXINE):
   TSH 3.2 mIU/L on levothyroxine 50 mcg/day — currently within normal range, but patient at risk for radiation-induced hypothyroidism with ongoing neck radiation changes. Baseline levothyroxine requirement may increase as the thyroid gland sustains late radiation injury.
   Plan: Continue levothyroxine 50 mcg/day; recheck TSH in 3 months; counsel patient to watch for symptoms of hypothyroidism (fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation).

10. OTOTOXICITY — GRADE 1 (BILATERAL HIGH-FREQUENCY HEARING LOSS):
    Stable as of last audiogram. Not progressing.
    Plan: Audiologic follow-up at 12 months post-treatment; patient counseled on potential for progressive late hearing changes; no intervention needed at this time.

11. FATIGUE:
    Grade 1-2; improving. Expected after combined modality therapy.
    Plan: Encourage gradual activity resumption; walking program; good sleep hygiene; ensure adequate nutrition via PEG.

FOLLOW-UP: Return to clinic in 3-4 weeks for clinical exam, labs (Mg, CBC, CMP, TSH), and to coordinate post-treatment imaging schedule. Patient provided contact information for urgent concerns. Modified barium swallow results to be reviewed at or before next visit.

Time spent: 45 minutes. Greater than 50% of encounter spent on counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed — Head and Neck Medical Oncology
